children.name: Upupa
children.description: One of the most striking birds of the Western Palearctic — salmon-pink with bold black-and-white barred wings and a dramatic fan-shaped crest raised in alarm or display. The oop-oop-oop call carries far over open Swedish landscapes. An irregular visitor to the south, with occasional breeding records in warm years.

children.name: Upupa epops
children.description: An unmistakeable pinkish-brown bird with bold black-and-white wings and a fan-shaped crest raised in excitement. A scarce but regular visitor to southern Sweden, probing lawns and bare ground for insect larvae.

children.name: Upupa africana
children.description: Long treated as a subspecies of the Eurasian Hoopoe, the African Hoopoe is now recognised as a full species on the basis of its deeper chestnut-rufous plumage, fully black-tipped crest feathers and a distinctly different, more nasal call. It is widespread and common across sub-Saharan Africa south of the Sahel, favouring open woodland, savanna with scattered trees, gardens and golf courses. It probes the ground with its long decurved bill for beetle larvae, ants and other invertebrates, and nests in natural tree holes or crevices in walls and termite mounds. Resident and largely non-migratory throughout its range.

children.name: Upupa marginata
children.description: An endemic species restricted to Madagascar, where it is the only hoopoe and is found island-wide in open and semi-open habitats from coastal scrub to forest edges and village gardens. Slightly smaller and more brightly coloured than the Eurasian Hoopoe, with a warmer rufous tone and a paler face; the crest is tipped black-and-white in the same dramatic fan pattern. Its three-note call differs from the European bird's insistent 'oop-oop-oop'. Like its relatives it feeds by probing soil and leaf litter for invertebrates. A familiar and beloved bird across Madagascar, it lacks the migratory restlessness of its Palearctic cousin.
